description: Architectural patterns for structuring UMG UI in UE5 — HUD, menus, widgets, and the separation between UI and game logic

entries.title: Pattern: HUD & UI Architecture
entries.description: How to structure your game's UI layer so widgets don't become tangled with gameplay code. Covers the separation between UI display, UI management, and game state.
entries.whatItIs: A clean architecture for UMG UI: widgets only display data and capture input, a HUD or UI manager handles widget lifecycle (create, show, hide, destroy), and game logic never lives inside widgets.
whenToUse: Any game with a HUD (health bar, minimap, ammo count) and menus (pause, inventory, settings)
When multiple widgets need to react to the same game state changes
When you want designers to build widget layouts in UMG without touching game logic
whenNotToUse: Extremely simple UI with one or two text elements — just create them directly on the HUD class
UI-only applications (tools, editors) where the widget IS the application
keyPoints: STEP 1 — Separate your UI into layers: HUD layer (always visible: health, ammo, minimap), Menu layer (overlays: pause, inventory, shop), and Popup layer (confirmations, tooltips, notifications).
STEP 2 — Create a UI Manager. This can be a UObject on the PlayerController, a Subsystem, or a function library. It handles: creating widgets, pushing/popping menus, managing input mode switches.

STEP 3 — Widgets are DUMB. They display data and fire events when clicked/pressed. They do NOT: query game state directly, cast to the player character, modify game variables, or call gameplay functions.

STEP 4 — Data flows one way: Game State -> Widget. Use one of these: (A) Widget exposes a SetData function that the controller calls, (B) Widget binds to a delegate on a component/subsystem, (C) Widget uses property bindings to a ViewModel.

STEP 5 — User actions flow the other way: Widget -> Controller. When a button is pressed, the widget broadcasts a delegate or calls a function on the controller. The widget says 'button was pressed', the controller decides what to do.
STEP 6 — Input mode management: when a menu opens, switch to UI input mode. When it closes, switch back to Game input mode. The UI Manager handles this, not individual widgets.

STEP 7 — For the HUD: create a main HUD widget (W_HUD) that contains sub-widgets (W_HealthBar, W_AmmoCounter, W_Minimap). Add it to viewport once in BeginPlay. Sub-widgets update through delegates from their respective components.
commonMistakes: Widgets casting to the Player Character to read health — the widget should receive health through a delegate or SetData call, not by reaching into gameplay code
Creating and destroying widgets every time a menu opens/closes — create once, show/hide with SetVisibility or Add/RemoveFromParent
Putting game logic in widget button callbacks — the widget should just broadcast 'ResumeClicked', the controller handles unpausing
Not managing input mode — opening a menu without switching to UI input means the player can still move and shoot behind the menu
Having every widget independently find and bind to game objects — use a central data source or have the controller push data to widgets
Deep widget nesting without clear data flow — if a child widget 5 levels deep needs player data, pass it down or use a shared ViewModel, don't have it cast to the player
goodPractices: Widgets are visual only — they receive data and emit UI events, nothing else
One HUD widget as the container, sub-widgets for each element — modular and easy to toggle
Use delegates to push data TO widgets: OnHealthChanged -> W_HealthBar::UpdateHealth
UI Manager controls widget lifecycle and input modes — widgets don't manage themselves
For menus: push/pop pattern. Opening Inventory pushes it onto a stack, pressing back pops it. Clean navigation.
Keep widget animations in the widget (UMG animations) — they're visual concern, not game logic
Use widget Switchers (UWidgetSwitcher) for tab-based UIs instead of creating/destroying child widgets
